summary: I was born in Vang Vieng, Laos in 1955. This is where my early life began before my family moved to Vientiane in 1979. Vang Vieng holds a special place in my heart as my hometown and the origin of my family's journey.

Laos is where my family lived before we became refugees. We moved from Vang Vieng to Vientiane in 1979, where my father got married. Laos is the country that shaped much of our early life before we had to leave for Thailand and eventually the United States.

In 1979, my family moved to Vientiane, Laos, where my father got married. It was an important chapter in our lives before the political situation forced us to seek refuge elsewhere. Vientiane was where my father started his own family and laid the foundation for our future.

Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp in Saraburi, Thailand was our home from 1993 until 2004. It was there that several of my younger siblings and I were born and raised. The camp was a place of refuge and hope for a better life before we finally moved to the United States.

Saraburi, Thailand is where the Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp is located, and it became our temporary home starting in 1993. We lived there as refugees for over a decade, waiting for the chance to start a new life. Saraburi holds memories of both hardship and hope for my family.

My family and I immigrated to the United States on September 16, 2004, settling in Saint Paul, Minnesota. This move was a major turning point, offering us hope for a better life after years in refugee camps. The United States became the place where my father carefully brought his qeej, symbolizing our culture and identity.

Saint Paul, Minnesota is where my family finally settled after leaving the refugee camp in Thailand. We arrived there in 2004, carrying our belongings and my father's treasured qeej. It became our new home where we hoped to build a better future.

My father, Vang Thao, was born in Vang Vieng, Laos in 1955 and has been a central figure in our family's journey. He learned to play the qeej from a young age and carried this instrument with him through all our migrations. His dedication to preserving our culture through the qeej is something I deeply respect and want to embrace myself.

The qeej is a traditional Hmong reed instrument that my father has played since he was five years old. It holds great cultural significance, being played at funerals and the Hmong New Year. My father wrapped his qeej carefully and brought it with him all the way from the refugee camp to the United States, showing how much it means to him.

I am part of the Hmong people, an ethnic group with rich traditions and culture. The qeej is one of our important cultural instruments, and playing it brings respect within our community. Our people have faced many challenges, including migration from Laos to refugee camps and eventually to the United States, but we continue to honor our heritage.

The Hmong New Year is a significant event where the qeej is traditionally played to celebrate the upcoming year. It is a time of cultural pride and community gathering for us. My father’s skill with the qeej connects him to this important celebration and to our Hmong identity.
